title: Delete test rows
summary: Practice safely deleting test rows from the inventory_items table. Learn to preview affected rows before deleting, use WHERE clauses to avoid accidental data loss, and confirm your changes.

sketchBlocks.title: Why preview before deleting?
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Deleting rows from a table is permanent—once removed, the data cannot be easily recovered. That's why it's a best practice to preview the rows you intend to delete using a SELECT statement with the same WHERE clause you plan to use in your DELETE. For example:

```sql
SELECT * FROM inventory_items WHERE is_test = 1;
```

This shows all rows marked as test data. After confirming the preview, you can safely run:

```sql
DELETE FROM inventory_items WHERE is_test = 1;
```

This workflow helps prevent accidental deletion of important data.

sketchBlocks.title: The importance of WHERE in DELETE
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
A DELETE statement without a WHERE clause removes **all** rows from the table. To avoid catastrophic data loss, always include a WHERE clause that targets only the rows you intend to remove. For example:

```sql
DELETE FROM inventory_items WHERE id = 2;
```

This deletes only the row with `id = 2`. Double-check your WHERE condition before executing the DELETE.

quizDraft.title: Preview test rows before deleting
quizDraft.prompt: Write a SQL query to preview all rows in the inventory_items table that are marked as test data (is_test = 1).
quizDraft.hint: Use SELECT with a WHERE clause to filter for test rows.

help.concept: Before deleting, preview the rows using SELECT and the same WHERE condition you plan to use in DELETE.
help.hint_1: Write a SELECT statement that filters for is_test = 1.
help.hint_2: Use SELECT * FROM inventory_items WHERE is_test = 1 to see all test rows.

quizDraft.title: Delete all test rows
quizDraft.prompt: Write a SQL statement to delete all rows from inventory_items where is_test is 1.
quizDraft.hint: Use DELETE with a WHERE clause to target test rows.

help.concept: DELETE statements remove rows matching the WHERE condition. Without WHERE, all rows would be deleted.
help.hint_1: Write DELETE FROM inventory_items WHERE is_test = 1.
help.hint_2: Make sure your WHERE clause matches only test rows.

quizDraft.title: Delete a specific test row by id
quizDraft.prompt: Write a SQL statement to delete the test row with id = 4 from inventory_items.

help.concept: To delete a specific row, use a WHERE clause that matches only that row's id.
help.hint_1: Write DELETE FROM inventory_items WHERE id = 4.
help.hint_2: Check that only the row with id = 4 is deleted.

quizDraft.starterCode: -- Delete the test row with id = 4

quizDraft.title: Why preview before deleting?
quizDraft.prompt: Which of the following are good reasons to preview rows before running a DELETE statement? Select all that apply.
quizDraft.hint: Think about safety and preventing mistakes.

help.concept: Previewing helps you confirm which rows will be deleted and avoid accidental data loss.
help.hint_1: Previewing lets you double-check your WHERE clause.
help.hint_2: It helps prevent deleting important data by mistake.

options.a: To confirm the WHERE clause matches only the intended rows.
options.b: To avoid accidentally deleting important data.
options.c: To make the DELETE statement run faster.
options.d: To see what data will be removed before it's gone.

quizDraft.title: Fill in the blank: DELETE with WHERE
quizDraft.prompt: Complete the SQL statement to delete only test rows from inventory_items.
quizDraft.hint: The WHERE clause should match test rows.

help.concept: A WHERE clause in DELETE limits which rows are removed.
help.hint_2: Use WHERE is_test = 1 to target test rows.
